metadata.title:
WHERE EVERY HOME FEELS


RELENTLESS GRIP OF LAW

pages.raw_text:
Loewee also attached the saw
ings of many union men in the
banks. That was a terrible blow,
in strike times, with grocery bills
owing.


The national union came to the
relief Of those whose deposits
were attached. and paid them
their money, giving a bond for it's
individual members.


"If you don't stop releasing these
attachments We'll make new at
tachments every day. You'll have
to give new bonds every day in
that case"


So the national union stopped.


1T doesn't cost anything to issue
an attachment in Connecticut
says Howard Taylor. counsel for
the Danbury unions. "Any lawyer
can make out one in his office and
serve it, without going to court.
But it coses money to give bonds,
and even John d. Rockefeller
couldn't give daily bonds for
100000 very long"


The manufacturers haven't been
entirely unkind. The might have
prevented the national union from
releasing the attached bank &C.
counts. But the manufacturers re-
fused to take full advantage of the
rights the law gave them. And
that helped Danbury to see that it
was not entirely case of man
against man, in the fight of capital
against unions, but Of system
against system, with bad laws ia
voring capital.


So, if there is any hard feeling
at all, it is against lawmakers who
bow down to capital, with its
wealth, instead of to labor, with it's
hopes.


OF 1000 men in the fine little city
Of Danbury, 1000 are now out of
employment. They are we'll dressed
and they all wear fine, new hats
(they can buy fine hats in the fac
tories for 81. You can see them
lounging about the elm shaded
streets. They are not saloon hang
ers on.


"Unionism has made this the fin
est little city in the country" said
Mayor Gilbert. These men get
from Sc to $40 a day, working from
$50 to 2 or 8 o'clock every day.
Unionism did that. But. If the light
is bright, nthat respect, the dark
side is all the darker. When the
unions do have trouble it is real
trouble. Everybody suffers"


"The day before the strike was
ordered a storekeeper ordered 5000
cans of corn from me-" said a trav-
cling man from New York, in the
hotel "The day after the strike
was ordered he Cancelled the or
der.


Somebody's going to miss that
corn in this town, simply because
he won't have the money to buy
it," said the salesman. Maybe it
means that women and children
will go hungry
